--- Page 1 ---
Quantum Optimization in Loc(Q)ation Science:
QUBO Formulations, Benchmark Problems, and a
Computational Study
Felix P. Broesamle1* and Stefan Nickel1*
1Institute for Operations Research, Karlsruhe Institute of Technology,
Karlsruhe, 76131, Germany.
*Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): felix.broesamle@kit.edu;
stefan.nickel@kit.edu;
Abstract
Recent advances in quantum computing and the increasing availability of quan-
tum hardware have substantially enhanced the practical relevance of quantum
approaches to discrete optimization. Among these, the Quadratic Unconstrained
Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulation provides a unifying modeling frame-
work for a broad class of NP-hard problems and is naturally suited to quantum
computing and quantum-inspired algorithms. Location science, network design,
and logistics represent core application domains of discrete optimization, com-
bining high practical impact with substantial computational challenges. In this
work, we develop QUBO formulations for several fundamental problems in these
domains, including a nonlinear integer formulation of the Discrete Ordered
Median Problem (DOMP). Beyond their modeling relevance, these QUBO for-
mulations serve as representative benchmark problems for assessing quantum
algorithms and quantum hardware. We further derive a tight bound for the
penalty parameter ensuring equivalence between the QUBO formulation and
its underlying integer program. Finally, we conduct a comprehensive compu-
tational study using QAOA, WS-QAOA, and classical heuristics for QUBO
instances of the p-Median Problem and the Fixed-Charge Facility Location Prob-
lem (FCFLP), and introduce two effective warm-start strategies for WS-QAOA
based on its linear programming relaxation.
Keywords: Location Science, Quantum Computing, Quantum Algorithms, Quantum
Optimization, Benchmark Problems, Discrete Optimization, Combinatorial
Optimization, Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO)
1
arXiv:2602.10951v1  [quant-ph]  11 Feb 2026


--- Page 2 ---
1 Introduction
Solving discrete optimization problems is a cornerstone of decision-making across
numerous domains, particularly in location science, network design, and logistics. The
optimization problems arising in these areas are often NP-hard, making them com-
putationally difficult to solve using classical exact methods; indeed the development
of integer programming theory and methods has been foundational in this context,
see e.g., [1], [2], [3], [4], [5] and [6]. As a result, there is growing interest in exploring
approaches that may offer advantages over classical deterministic approaches, e.g.,
heuristics and metaheuristics. One such promising paradigm is quantum computing,
which leverages the principles of quantum mechanics to potentially accelerate the
solution of combinatorial optimization problems.
Many quantum optimization methods are based on the Quantum Adiabatic Theorem,
see [7] and [8]. This theorem asserts that a quantum system initialized in the ground
state of a simple Hamiltonian will remain in the instantaneous ground state as the
Hamiltonian is slowly evolved into a more complex one, provided the evolution is
sufficiently slow and certain spectral-gap conditions hold. By encoding an optimiza-
tion problem into the final Hamiltonian, the system ideally transitions into the
optimal solution state at the end of the adiabatic evolution. This principle forms the
foundation of Adiabatic Quantum Computing (AQC), see e.g., [9], and underlies the
operational mechanism of contemporary quantum annealers. It also motivates the
design of the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) [10], which
can be interpreted as a discretized analogue of the adiabatic evolution and is imple-
mentable on gate-based quantum computers.
A class of problems particularly well-suited for such quantum devices are those
expressible as instances of the Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization Prob-
lem (QUBO). The QUBO formulation enables a wide range of discrete optimization
problems to be reformulated as energy-minimization tasks, thereby aligning naturally
with the operational principles of both quantum annealers and QAOA. In [11], QUBO
formulations are presented for the 21 NP-complete problems from KarpŌĆÖs list [12].
However, despite rapid technological advances and increasing attention, a significant
gap remains in the availability of well-structured, practically relevant benchmark
problems for assessing the capabilities of quantum algorithms and hardware [13].
Benchmarks must not only reflect theoretical complexity but also capture the combi-
natorial structure observed in real-world applications.
In the domain of location science, network design and logistics, classical modeling
and solution techniques are well established, see e.g., [6]; however, to the best of
our knowledge, optimization problems arising in location science have not yet been
investigated using their QUBO formulations and quantum optimization algorithms.
In this work, we aim to help to close this gap by providing QUBO formulations for
a collection of fundamental and computationally challenging problems from location
science, network design, and logistics. Specifically, we consider the p-Median Prob-
lem, the p-Center Problem, the Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem (FCFLP),
the Generalized Assignment Problem (GAP), the Discrete Ordered Median Problem
(DOMP), and the Discrete Multi-Period Facility Location Problem. These problems
are not only of high practical relevance but are also NP-hard in general, making
2


--- Page 3 ---
them well suited as benchmark problems for quantum optimization.
The choice of the penalty parameter in a QUBO formulation, when constraints are
incorporated into the objective function, is critical. To address this, in Section 3 we
propose a penalty parameter that ensures equivalence between the QUBO and its
underlying integer program.
Additionally, we conduct a comprehensive computational study in Section 4 to
evaluate the performance of current quantum algorithms on the proposed instances.
We consider both QAOA [10] and WS-QAOA [14], where WS-QAOA is a variant
of the standard QAOA incorporating warm-start initialization. For the warm-start,
a common approach is to consider the continuous relaxation or the semidefinite
programming (SDP) relaxation of the QUBO [14]. However, since the continuous
relaxation yields in general a nonconvex continuous optimization problem, computing
a global minimum is NP-hard; in practice, only a local minimum of the continuous
relaxation can be obtained efficiently. Similarly, the SDP relaxation is not necessarily
tight and may therefore fail to produce high-quality warm-start points in general.
To overcome this limitation, we propose two linear programming (LP) based warm-
start strategies for the FCFLP in Section 4.2.3 (FCFLP) and 4.2.4. The numerical
experiments show that these warm-start strategies yield high-quality starting points,
outperforming both the continuous relaxation and the SDP-based warm-start strate-
gies. Furthermore, the proposed warm-start approaches can also be applied to QUBOs
derived from general integer programs.
The results of the computational study provide insights into the current state and
limitations of quantum optimization technology. Moreover, in the computational
experiments we consider the aggregated and disaggregated formulation of the FCFLP,
as we broadly ask how the polyhedral formulation of the original optimization prob-
lem impacts the solutions obtained by quantum optimization algorithms when using
QUBO formulations.
Quantum optimization algorithms, such as QAOA, have attracted significant atten-
tion, but are currently limited by Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices
[15ŌĆō17], which suffer from a limited number of qubits, restricted connectivity, and
significant noise, often dominating algorithmic performance. To isolate algorithmic
behavior, we perform all quantum experiments using the Qiskit AerSimulator
[18, 19] in a noise-free configuration, which computes the exact evolution of quantum
states under prescribed gate operations.
Related works: QUBO formulations have been widely studied as a unifying model-
ing framework for combinatorial optimization. A general introduction and systematic
methodology for deriving QUBOs from integer programs, along with their applica-
tion to various combinatorial problems, is provided in [20]. An overview of industrial
applications of quantum computing and quantum optimization is presented in [21].
Several studies address financial applications, such as portfolio optimization [22ŌĆō24].
Reference [25] reviews quantum technologies for discrete optimization and QUBO
formulations for combinatorial problems. Further examples include QUBO formu-
lations for the Job Shop Scheduling Problem [26], the binary knapsack problem
3


--- Page 4 ---
[27], and routing problems [28ŌĆō30]. A comprehensive catalogue of QUBO and Ising
formulations for classical NP-hard problems is presented in [11], specifically the 21
NP-hard problems listed by Karp [12].
Our contribution: In this work, we introduce QUBO formulations for classical opti-
mization problems arising in location science, network design, and logistics, including
the p-Median Problem, the p-Center Problem, the Fixed-Charge-Facility Location
Problem (FCFLP), the Discrete Ordered Median Problem (DOMP), and the Dis-
crete Multi-Period Facility Location Problem. In doing so, we extend the benchmark
problems in the field of quantum optimization to include practically highly relevant
problems, which are also NP-hard and challenging to solve. Furthermore, we propose
a penalty parameter selection in Theorem 2, ensuring that a global optimum of the
QUBO formulation corresponds to a global optimum of the underlying integer opti-
mization problem. In the computational study, we investigate the newly introduced
QUBO formulations for the p-Median Problem and the FCFLP applying QAOA, WS-
QAOA, and classical heuristics. Additionally, we introduce two promising LP-based
warm-start strategies for the FCFLP for WS-QAOA, which are also applicable for
QUBOs arising from general integer programs. Finally, by presenting the aggregated
and disaggregated formulation of the FCFLP together with the corresponding QUBO
formulations, as well as the numerical evaluation of both formulations, we aim to
draw attention to the need for further research on how the polyhedral properties of
integer programs influence the behavior of quantum algorithms.
The structure of this paper is as follows: in Section 2, we introduce the fundamental
concepts of quantum optimization, including the quantum optimization algorithms
QAOA and WS-QAOA, as well as the basic principles of location science. In Section
3, we present QUBO formulations for the aforementioned optimization problems.
Finally, in Section 4, we provide comprehensive numerical experiments.
2 Preliminaries
In this section, we introduce the fundamental concepts of quantum adiabatic opti-
mization, Ising Hamiltonians, and their equivalent QUBO formulations, which form
the theoretical foundation for quantum optimization methods. We further present the
gate-based quantum algorithms QAOA and WS-QAOA, which are employed in the
numerical experiments in Section 4. Finally, we provide a brief overview of location
science.
2.1 Quantum Adiabatic Optimization, Ising Hamiltonian, and
QUBOs
Quantum Adiabatic Optimization leverages the adiabatic theorem of quantum
mechanics [7, 8] to solve combinatorial optimization problems [9]. The process ini-
tializes a quantum system in the ground state of a simple Hamiltonian H0, then
gradually and slowly evolves it to the problem (cost) Hamiltonian HP , whose ground
state encodes the optimal solution [11]. The time-dependent Hamiltonian is typically
4


--- Page 5 ---
defined as
H(t) =

1 ŌłÆt
T

H0 + t
T HP ,
t Ōłł[0, T],
where T denotes the total evolution time. Provided the evolution is slow enough
and spectral gaps do not close, the state of the system remains in the instantaneous
ground state throughout the process. Measuring the system at t = T yields an opti-
mal solution with high probability, or even with probability 1, if T is large enough.
HP is chosen as the quantum analogue of a classical Ising Hamiltonian,
HP (z1, ..., zn) = ŌłÆ
X
i<j
Jijzizj ŌłÆ
X
i
hizi,
with spin variable zi Ōłł{ŌłÆ1, +1}, and real-valued pairwise couplings Jij and local
fields hi. In the quantum setting, the spin variables zi are replaced by Pauli-Z opera-
tors acting on the i-th qubit, respectively. The initial Hamiltonian H0 = ŌłÆh0
P
i Xi
induces a transverse field, producing a uniform superposition over all computational
basis states, where Xi denotes the Pauli-X operator acting on the i-th qubit.
Minimizing the classical Ising energy is equivalent to solving a Quadratic Uncon-
strained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem via the substitution zi = 2xi ŌłÆ1
with xi Ōłł{0, 1}. This yields
min
xŌłł{0,1}n
xT Qx + qT x + c,
(1)
with suitable Q ŌłłRn├Śn, q ŌłłRn, and c ŌłłR, providing a discrete optimization problem
naturally compatible with quantum optimization algorithms based on the quantum
adiabatic theorem.
2.2 Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA)
The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), originally proposed by
[10], is a quantum-classical hybrid algorithm for solving combinatorial optimization
problems. Conceptually, QAOA is inspired by quantum adiabatic optimization, as it
discretizes the continuous adiabatic evolution into a sequence of alternating unitaries.
In contrast to quantum annealing, however, QAOA is specifically designed for gate-
based, universal quantum computers.
QAOA depends on two sets of real-valued parameters,
╬│ = (╬│1, ..., ╬│p),
and
╬▓ = (╬▓1, ..., ╬▓p),
where the depth parameter (number of layers) p ŌłłN controls both the expressive
power of the variational ansatz and the number of alternating operator layers. The
algorithm starts from the uniform superposition state |+Ō¤®ŌŖŚn and alternates between
5


--- Page 6 ---
applying the cost (problem) Hamiltonian HC and a mixing Hamiltonian HM, yielding
the variational quantum state
|Žł(╬▓, ╬│)Ō¤®= eŌłÆi╬▓pHM eŌłÆi╬│pHC ┬Ę ┬Ę ┬Ę eŌłÆi╬▓1HM eŌłÆi╬│1HC|+Ō¤®ŌŖŚn.
The cost (problem) Hamiltonian HC encodes the objective function of the underlying
QUBO problem
HC|xŌ¤®= C(x)|xŌ¤®,
ŌłĆx Ōłł{0, 1}n,
where C(x) denotes the QUBO cost function. The mixing Hamiltonian HM is defined
as
HM =
n
X
i=1
Xi,
where Xi denotes the Pauli-X operator acting on the i-th qubit.
For a fixed parameter pair (╬▓, ╬│), the quantum circuit implementing the above uni-
tary generates a distribution over computational basis states, i.e., over points in
{0, 1}n. Sampling from this distribution yields candidate solutions to the QUBO. The
parameters are optimized classically to minimize the expected value
Ō¤©Žł(╬▓, ╬│)|HC|Žł(╬▓, ╬│)Ō¤®,
which serves as the variational estimate of the problemŌĆÖs objective function.
Proposition 1 ([25], Remark 1) An arbitrary QUBO instance with N variables that is solved
with QAOA requires N physical qubits. While transpilation might affect the ouput quality, it
does not necessarily require additional physical qubits.
2.3 Warm-Start Quantum Approximate Optimization
Algorithm (WS-QAOA)
Warm-Start QAOA (WS-QAOA), introduced by [14], is a variant of QAOA that
incorporates problem-specific information into both the initial state and the mixing
Hamiltonian. In contrast to the standard QAOA, which starts from the uniform super-
position |+Ō¤®ŌŖŚn, WS-QAOA initializes the quantum circuit using a warm-start point
x0 ŌłłRn.
The WS-QAOA circuit is initialized in the state
|ŽĢ0Ō¤®=
nŌłÆ1
O
i=0

While WS-QAOA can improve convergence and solution quality by biasing the search
toward promising regions of the solution space, it may encounter reachability issues
when the warm-start point x0 has integral components, which differ from the global
optimal solution xŌłŚof (1), i.e., when x0
i = 0 and xŌłŚ
i = 1, or vice versa.
A practical strategy to mitigate this issue is to construct a modified warm-start point
┬»x0 by projecting x0 onto the ŌäōŌł×-norm ball centered at ( 1
2, ..., 1
2) ŌłłRn with radius
r = 0.5 ŌłÆ╬Ą for ╬Ą Ōłł(0, 0.5]. This projection ensures that all components of ┬»x0 satisfy
┬»x0
i Ōłł[╬Ą, 1 ŌłÆ╬Ą] for all i = 1, ..., n.
2.4 Location Science
Location science is a well-established research field concerned with determining the
optimal placement of facilities to serve a given set of demand points. Since its origins
in the classical works [31] and [32], the field has evolved into a core area of operations
research, combining elements of mathematics, economics, geography and computer
science [4ŌĆō6, 33]. Over the past decades, location science has developed a rich theo-
retical foundation and a wide range of modeling and solution approaches, motivated
by both methodological advances and practical applications in diverse domains [34].
Discrete location problems, in particular, have received extensive attention due to
their practical relevance in logistics, transportation, network design and public ser-
vice planning. These problems typically involve selecting a subset of candidate sites
for facility placement while minimizing or maximizing a given objective function that
represents costs, distances, or service quality [34].
In the discrete setting, a finite set of candidate sites is available for facility placement,
and a finite set of clients requires service from these facilities. Let I = {1, ..., n} denote
the set of potential sites, which, without loss of generality, I is assumed to coincide
with the set of clients J. The objective is typically to decide which sites to open and
how to assign clients to open facilities according to a specified cost or distance metric.
7


--- Page 8 ---
3 Optimization Problems arising in Location Science
as QUBOs
In this section, we present QUBO formulations for the p-Median Problem (Section
3.2), the p-Center Problem (Section 3.3), the Fixed-Charge Facility Location Prob-
lem (FCFLP, Section 3.4), the Generalized Assignment Problem (GAP, Section
3.5), the Discrete Ordered Median Problem (DOMP, Section 3.6), and the Discrete
Multi-Period Facility Location Problem (Section 3.7). We consider the general setting
described above in Section 2.4. Before deriving the QUBO formulations, Section
3.1 establishes a result that yields a tight penalty parameter ensuring equivalence
between an integer program and its QUBO formulation.
We follow standard practice in the literature, see, e.g., [11], and present each QUBO
formulation (1) in terms of the corresponding cost Hamiltonian written in binary
variables. The corresponding upper triangular (or symmetric) QUBO matrix can then
be obtained by computing the linear and quadratic coefficients of the Hamiltonian.
3.1 Formulating QUBOs and Choice of Penalty Parameter
We consider general integer programs (IP) of the form
min
x
╦£cT x,
(2a)
s.t.
╦£Ax = ╦£b,
(2b)
Bx Ōēżd,
(2c)
l Ōēżx Ōēżu,
(2d)
x ŌłłZ╦£n,
(2e)
where ╦£c ŌłłR╦£n, ╦£A ŌłłZm1├Ś╦£n, B ŌłłZm2├Ś╦£n,╦£b ŌłłZm1, d ŌłłZm2, and l, u ŌłłZ╦£n.
A common first step in constructing a QUBO formulation (1) is to reformulate (2)
into an equivalent binary program of the form
min
x
cT x
(3a)
s.t.
Ax = b,
(3b)
gi(x) Ōēż0,
ŌłĆi = 1, ..., r,
(3c)
x Ōłł{0, 1}n,
(3d)
where c ŌłłRn, A ŌłłZm├Śn, b ŌłłZm, and gi : {0, 1}n ŌåÆZ, are simple integer-valued
constraint functions, for which direct penalty-terms exist, see Table 1.
Inequality constraints in (2) are first transformed into equality constraints by intro-
ducing integer slack variables or kept as simple constraints gi. Subsequently, each
integer variable is represented using a binary encoding.
Now, to obtain a QUBO formulation, the constraints of (3) are removed and their
violation in the objective function penalized. The general linear equality constraints
8


--- Page 9 ---
Ax = b are incorporated into the objective via row-wise quadratic penality terms, i.e,
(bi ŌłÆAix)2,
i = 1, ..., m,
where Ai denotes the i-th row of A and any violation of constraint i yields at least a
value of 1. For each constraint gi(x) Ōēż0, we associate a penalty function pi(x), defined
such that any violation produces an integer-valued penalty of at least one. See Table
1 for the penalty-terms.
Table 1: Table of simple constraints
and corresponding penalty terms
Constraint
Penalty term
xi + xj Ōēż1
P ┬Ę xixj
xi Ōēżxj
P ┬Ę xi(1 ŌłÆxj)
x1 + ┬Ę ┬Ę ┬Ę + xn Ōēż1
P ┬Ę PnŌłÆ1
i=1
Pn
j=i+1 xixj
We now present the penalized quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO)
formulation of (3), expressed here as an alternative to the classical matrix-based
representation (1):
min
xŌłł{0,1}n f(x) = cT x + P ┬Ę
r
X
i=1
pi(x) + P ┬Ę
m
X
i=1
(bi ŌłÆAix)2,
(4)
where P > 0 is a penalty parameter.
The choice of the penalty parameter P is critical for both theoretical and practical
performance, analogously to classical penalty methods in nonlinear programming, see,
e.g., [35]. P must be sufficiently large to ensure that any global minimizer of (4) is
feasible for (3), yet not so large as to induce numerical instability or an ill-conditioned
energy landscape.
We next provide tight conditions on the penalty parameter that guarantee equiva-
lence between the QUBO formulation (4) and the binary program (3), and therefore
the integer program (2).
Theorem 2 Consider the optimization problem (2), its equivalent formulation (3), and the
associated QUBO formulation (4), and assume that (2) is feasible. Let c ŌłłRn denote the
cost vector of (3). If the penalty parameter in (4) satisfies P > Pn
i=1|ci|, then every global
minimizer of the optimization problem (4) is feasible for (2) and is also a global minimizer
of (2).
9


--- Page 10 ---
Proof Let P > Pn
i=1|ci|. We show the statement for (3); the result for (2) then follows
immediately from their equivalence. Let Ōłģ╠Ė= X = {x Ōłł{0, 1}n : Ax = b, gi(x) Ōēż0, i =
1, ..., r} denote the feasible set of (3). We set CŌłÆ= Pn
i=1:ci<0|ci| and C+ = Pn
i=1:ci>0 ci.
Then, for any x Ōłł{0, 1}n, we have cT x Ōłł{ŌłÆCŌłÆ, ..., C+}.
Let yŌłŚbe a global minimizer of (4). We show by contradiction that yŌłŚis feasible for (3).
Therefore, suppose that yŌłŚis infeasible for (3). Then, it holds
r

m

(5)
and we obtain

This is a contradiction to the global optimality of yŌłŚ. Now, as yŌłŚis feasible, we conclude by
the global optimality of yŌłŚ:

Corollary 1 Consider the optimization problem (2), its equivalent formulation (3), and the
associated QUBO formulation (4), and assume that (2) is feasible. Let c ŌłłRn, with c Ōēź0,
denote the cost vector of (3). If the penalty parameter in (4) satisfies P > Pn
i=1 ci, then
every global minimizer of the optimization problem (4) is feasible for (2) and is also a global
minimizer of (2).
Proof This follows directly from Theorem 2.
Ō¢Ī
For certain structured instances of (2) and (3), smaller penalty parameters
may suffice, particularly if additional problem-specific information, preprocessing, or
relaxation-based estimates are available. We note that, for several combinatorial prob-
lems, [25] provides a problem-specific selection of a tight penalty parameter. A similar
proof strategy was employed in [30] to verify that the penalty parameter from Theorem
2 is valid for a vehicle routing problem with time windows.
Henceforth, we assume that all considered optimization problems are feasible.
3.2 p-Median Problem
The p-Median Problem is a classical optimization problem in location science, first
formalized in [36]. The objective is to select p facility locations, called medians, from
a set of candidate sites to minimize the total distance or cost between demand points
and their assigned facilities, see also [37]. Let dj > 0, j ŌłłJ denote the demand of each
client, and cij Ōēź0 the cost of serving client j ŌłłJ from facility i ŌłłI. From now on, we
assume I = J = {1, ..., n}, so that there are n potential facility locations and p < n.
To formulate the p-Median Problem, we introduce the following binary variables:
10


--- Page 11 ---
ŌĆó yi = 1, if a facility is placed at site i ŌłłI and 0 otherwise.
ŌĆó xij = 1, if client j ŌłłJ is assigned to a facility located at i ŌłłI and 0 otherwise.
The p-Median Problem is formulated as the following optimization problem:
min
x,y
X
iŌłłI
X
jŌłłJ
djcijxij
(6a)
s.t.
X
iŌłłI
xij = 1,
ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
(6b)
xij Ōēżyi,
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(6c)
X
iŌłłI
yi = p,
(6d)
xij Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(6e)
yi Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI.
(6f)
Here, the first condition ensures that every client is assigned to exactly one facility.
The second constraint links the allocation and facility variables, ensuring that a client
can only be assigned to an open facility. The third constraint ensures the choice of
exactly p facilities.
Example 1 We consider the p-Median Problem with n = 4 potential facility locations. The
input data are given by the assignment cost matrix C ŌłłR4├Ś4 and the demand vector d ŌłłR4:
C =
’Ż½
’Ż¼
’Ż¼
’ŻŁ
2 11 13 6
14 0 15 11
5 14 1
6
5 12 15 2
’ŻČ
’ŻĘ
’ŻĘ
’ŻĖ,
d =
’Ż½
’Ż¼
’Ż¼
’ŻŁ
4
4
13
11
’ŻČ
’ŻĘ
’ŻĘ
’ŻĖ
For p = 2, the optimal solution opens the facilities 2 and 3, i.e., y2 = y3 = 1, while y1 =
y4 = 0. The corresponding client-facility assignments are x22 = x31 = x33 = x34 = 1, with
all remaining xij = 0. The resulting optimal objective value is 99.
QUBO formulation: The cost Hamiltonian for (6) is given by
H =
X
iŌłłI
X
jŌłłJ
djcijxij
(7a)
+ P

jŌłłJ
 
1 ŌłÆ

iŌłłI

p ŌłÆ

iŌłłI
yi

(7d)
11


--- Page 12 ---
The p-Median Problem has the appealing property that no auxiliary variables or
constraints are required to express it as a QUBO (1).
Lemma 1 The p-Median Problem (6) can be formulated as a QUBO with np-Median =
|I||J| + |I| binary variables. Therefore, the QUBO formulation (7) can be solved on a gate-
based Quantum Computer with np-Median physical qubits via QAOA or WS-QAOA.
Proof In the QUBO formulation (7), only the binary variables xij for i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ, and yi for
i ŌłłI, are used, giving a total of np-Median = |I||J| + |I| binary variables. The second part of
the statement follows directly from Proposition 1.
Ō¢Ī
Corollary 2 Let the penalty parameter in (7) be P = P
jŌłłJ
P
iŌłłI djcij +1. Then, any global
minimizer of (7) yields a global minimizer of (6).
Proof The p-Median Problem (6) is already formulated in the form (3), with objective
function P
jŌłłJ
P
iŌłłI djcijxij. Consequently, the statement follows from Corollary 1.
Ō¢Ī
Example 2 We consider Example 1. The penalty parameter is given by P = 1100. The
upper-triangular QUBO matrix is given as follows:

The offset for this QUBO problem is c = 8800.
3.3 p-Center Problem
Minimizing the total or average distance between clients and facilities, as in the p-
Median Problem, may not be an appropriate criterion for all types of facility location
decisions. Such measures tend to favor clients clustered in population centers, often at
the expense of those who are spatially dispersed. In contexts where equitable service
is desired, a criterion focusing on the worst-served clients is more appropriate, leading
to the p-Center Problem [36, 38, 39].
Let dij Ōēź0, i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ, denote the distance (or cost) from client j ŌłłJ to facility
12


--- Page 13 ---
i ŌłłI. We assume, without loss of generality, that dij ŌłłN0 for all i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ.
For the p-Center Problem, we introduce the binary variables:
ŌĆó yi = 1, if a facility is placed at site i ŌłłI and 0 otherwise.
ŌĆó xij = 1, if client j ŌłłJ is assigned to a facility located at i ŌłłI, and 0 otherwise.
The p-Center Problem can be formulated as
min
x,y,z
z
(8a)
s.t.
X
iŌłłI
xij = 1,
ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
(8b)
X
iŌłłI
dijxij Ōēżz,
ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
(8c)
xij Ōēżyi,
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(8d)
X
iŌłłI
yi Ōēżp,
(8e)
xij Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(8f)
yi Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI,
(8g)
z ŌłłZ.
(8h)
The objective function, together with the second constraint, ensures that the objec-
tive value z is greater than or equal to the maximum distance between any clients
and facilities they are assigned to. The first constraint guarantees that each client is
assigned to exactly one facility, while the third constraint links the allocation and
facility variables, ensuring that a client can only be assigned to an open facility. The
fourth constraint limits the number of facilities that can be opened to at most p.
QUBO formulation: Let dmax = maxi,j dij and dmin = mini,j dij. To transform
the inequality constraint P
iŌłłI dijxij Ōēżz for each j ŌłłJ, into an equality constraint,
we introduce an integral slack variable sj Ōłł{0, ..., dmax} for each j ŌłłJ, yielding
P
iŌłłI dijxij + sj = z. Next, we replace the integral variable z by binary variables.
We introduce binary variables zk Ōłł{0, 1}, k Ōłł{1, ..., Ōīłlog2(dmax ŌłÆdmin + 1)Ōīē} and
express z as
z = dmin +
Ōīłlog2(dmaxŌłÆdmin+1)Ōīē
X
k=1
2kŌłÆ1zk,
since
z
Ōēź
dmin
for
feasible
points.
Each
sj
is
represented
using
binary
variables sjk
Ōłł
{0, 1}, k
Ōłł
{1, ..., Ōīłlog2(dmax + 1)Ōīē} for each j
Ōłł
J, via
sj = PŌīłlog2(dmax+1)Ōīē
k=1
2kŌłÆ1sjk. To express the constraint P
iŌłłI yi Ōēżp as an equality
constraint, we introduce an integer slack variable u Ōłł{0, ..., p}. This slack variable is
then encoded in binary form using uk Ōłł{0, 1}, k Ōłł{1, ..., Ōīłlog2(p + 1)Ōīē}, such that
u = PŌīłlog2(p+1)Ōīē
k=1
2kŌłÆ1uk.
13


--- Page 14 ---
We set Ōäōz = Ōīłlog2(dmax ŌłÆdmin + 1)Ōīēand Ōäōs = Ōīłlog2(dmax + 1)Ōīē. Combining all
components yields the following cost Hamiltonian for (8):
H = dmin +
Ōäōz

k=1
2kŌłÆ1zk
(9a)

X

2kŌłÆ1uk
’ŻČ

2

(9e)
Lemma 2 The p-Center Problem (8) can be formulated as a QUBO with np-Center = |I||J|+
|I|+Ōīłlog2(dmaxŌłÆdmin+1)Ōīē+|J|Ōīłlog2(dmax+1)Ōīē+Ōīłlog2(p+1)Ōīēbinary variables. Therefore,
the QUBO formulation (9) can be solved on a gate-based Quantum Computer with np-Center
physical qubits via QAOA or WS-QAOA.
Proof In the QUBO formulation (9), the binary variables xij for i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ, yi for i ŌłłI,
and the auxiliary binary variables zk for k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(dmax ŌłÆdmin + 1)Ōīē, sjk for j Ōłł
J, k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(dmax + 1)Ōīē, and uk for k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(p + 1)Ōīēare used. Consequently,
the total number of binary variables is np-Center = |I||J| + |I| + Ōīłlog2(dmax ŌłÆdmin + 1)Ōīē+
|J|Ōīłlog2(dmax + 1)Ōīē+ Ōīłlog2(p + 1)Ōīē, and the second part of the statement follows from
Proposition 1.
Ō¢Ī
Corollary 3 Let the penalty parameter in (9) be P = PŌīłlog2(dmaxŌłÆdmin+1)Ōīē
i=1
2kŌłÆ1+1. Then,
any global minimizer of (9) yields a global minimizer of (8).
Proof For the p-Center Problem, the objective function of its equivalent formulation of the
form (3), used to construct the QUBO (9), is PŌīłlog2(dmaxŌłÆdmin+1)Ōīē
k=1
2kŌłÆ1zk. Consequently,
the statement follows from Corollary 1.
Ō¢Ī
3.4 Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem
The Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem (FCFLP) is a classical problem in
location science and operations research, in which two decisions must be made:
14


--- Page 15 ---
1. location decisions, determinig which facilities i ŌłłI to open, and
2. allocation decisions, specifying how client demands j ŌłłJ are served from the
opened facilities.
We assume I = J = {1, ..., n}. Each facility opening incurs a fixed (setup) cost,
while serving client demands generates variable assignment costs. The objective is to
determine a cost-minimizing selection of facilities to open and an assignment of clients
to those facilities that satisfies all demand and capacity constraints [3, 40]. The model
parameters are as follows: qi > 0 denotes the capacity of facility i ŌłłI, dj > 0 is the
demand of client j ŌłłJ, fi is the fixed cost of opening facility i, and cij Ōēź0 is the
cost of serving all demand of client j from facility i. We assume qi, dj ŌłłN for all
i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ.
For the formulation of the Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem we introduce the
binary decision variables:
ŌĆó yi = 1, if a facility is open at location i ŌłłI, and 0 otherwise.
ŌĆó xij = 1, if client j ŌłłJ is served by facility i ŌłłI, and 0 otherwise.
The Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem is then formulated as the following
optimization problem:
min
x,y
X
iŌłłI
fiyi +
X
iŌłłI
X
jŌłłJ
cijxij
(10a)
s.t.
X
iŌłłI
xij = 1,
ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
(10b)
X
jŌłłJ
djxij Ōēżqiyi
ŌłĆi ŌłłI,
(10c)
xij Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(10d)
yi Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI.
(10e)
The objective function consists of two components: the first term represents the total
fixed cost of opening facilities, and the second term accounts for the total assignment
(service) cost of allocating clients to open facilites. The constraints (10c) play a double
role: they ensure that no facility exceeds its capacity and simultaneously couples the
location and allocation decisions. The assignment constraints guarantee that each
client is served by exactly one open facility.
An alternative but equivalent formulation of (10) is given by
min
x,y
X
iŌłłI
fiyi +
X
iŌłłI
X
jŌłłJ
cijxij
(11a)
s.t.
X
iŌłłI
xij = 1,
ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
(11b)
X
jŌłłJ
djxij Ōēżqi
ŌłĆi ŌłłI,
(11c)
xij Ōēżyi,
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(11d)
15


--- Page 16 ---
xij Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(11e)
yj Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłI.
(11f)
In this formulation, the constraint (10c) is replaced by the two constraints (11c)
and (11d). Constraint (11c) ensures that the maximum capacity of a facility is not
exceeded, while constraint (11d) couples the variables x and y, thereby ensuring that
clients can only be assigned to open facilities.
We refer to (10) as the aggregated formulation and to (11) as the disaggregated
formulation.
Example 3 We consider the FCFLP with |I| = |J| = n = 3. The input data are given by the
assignment cost matrix C ŌłłR3├Ś3, the demand vector d ŌłłR3, the fixed cost vector f ŌłłR3,
and the maximum capacity vector q ŌłłR3:
C =
’Ż½
’ŻŁ
0 4 4
9 0 2
5 5 0
’ŻČ
’ŻĖ, d =
’Ż½
’ŻŁ
3
8
10
’ŻČ
’ŻĖ, f =
’Ż½
’ŻŁ
25
9
17
’ŻČ
’ŻĖ, q =
’Ż½
’ŻŁ
12
10
10
’ŻČ
’ŻĖ
The optimal solution opens the facilities 1 and 2, i.e., y1 = y2 = 1, while y3 = 0. The
corresponding client-facility assignments are x11 = x12 = x23 = 1, with all remaining xij = 0.
The resulting optimal objective value is 40.
The disaggregated formulation (11) is theoretically stronger than the aggregated
one (10), as the constraint set (11c) + (11d) dominates (10c) in the LP-relaxation.
Consequently, as discussed by [3], the disaggregated formulation typically yields
tighter bounds in a branch-and-cut algorithm. However, modern state-of-the-art
solvers can automatically detect and generate disaggregated constraints on the fly
whenever they are violated by the current feasible solution. Therefore, in practice,
the aggregated formulation is often preferable, as it is more compact and leads to
significantly smaller and faster to solve linear relaxations [3].
This is an interesting observation, and it raises the question of whether a similar
behavior can be observed for QUBO formulations, namely, whether one formulation
performs bettern than the other. Therefore, in the numerical experiments in Section
4, we consider and compare both the aggregated and the disaggregated formulations.
QUBO formulation: To transform the inequality constraints (10c) and (11c)
into equality constraints, we introduce integer slack variables ui Ōłł{0, ..., qi} for
each i ŌłłI. This yields the reformulated constraints P
jŌłłJ djxij + ui = qiyi and
P
jŌłłJ djxij + ui = qi for each i ŌłłI. For the binary encoding of each slack variable
ui, we define ki = Ōīłlog2(qi + 1)Ōīēand introduce binary variables zik Ōłł{0, 1} for
k Ōłł{1, ..., ki}. The integer variable ui is then represented as ui = Pki
k=1 2kŌłÆ1zik.
This yields the following cost Hamiltonians: for (10), it is given by
H =
X
iŌłłI
fiyi +
X
iŌłłI
X
jŌłłJ
cijxij
(12a)

--- Page 17 ---
+ P
X
jŌłłJ
 
1 ŌłÆ

xij
!2
(12b)
+ P
X
iŌłłI
’Ż½

djxij ŌłÆ

’ŻČ
’ŻĖ

,
(12c)
and for (11), it is given by

+ P
X

jŌłłJ
xij(1 ŌłÆyi).
(13d)
Lemma 3 The FCFLP can be formulated as a QUBO with nF CF LP = |I||J| + |I| +
P
iŌłłIŌīłlog2(qi + 1)Ōīēbinary variables. Therefore, the QUBO formulations (12) and (13) can
be solved on a gate-based Quantum Computer with nF CF LP physical qubits via QAOA or
WS-QAOA.
Proof Both QUBO formulations, (12) and (13), use the binary variables xij for i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
yi for i ŌłłI, and the auxiliary binary variables zik Ōłł{0, 1} for i ŌłłI, k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(qi + 1)Ōīē.
Consequently, the total number of binary variables is nF CF LP = |I||J|+|I|+P
iŌłłIŌīłlog2(qi+
1)Ōīē, and the second part of the statement follows from Proposition 1.
Ō¢Ī
Corollary 4 Let the penalty parameter in (12), (13) be P = P
iŌłłI fi + P
iŌłłI
P
jŌłłJ ccij + 1.
Then, any global minimizer of (12), (13) yields a global minimizer of (10), (11).
Proof For the FCFLP, the objective function of the equivalent formulations of the form (3),
used to construct both QUBOs (12) and (13), is P
iŌłłI fiyi + P
iŌłłI
P
jŌłłJ cijxij. Thus, the
statement is a consequence of Corollary 1.
Ō¢Ī
Example 4 We consider Example 3. The penalty parameter is given by P = 81. First, we
consider the QUBO formulation for (10). We first introduce the slack variables. It holds
Ōīłlog2(12 + 1)Ōīē= Ōīłlog2(10 + 1)Ōīē= Ōīłlog2(10 + 1)Ōīē= 4. Therefore, we introduce the vari-
ables s11, ..., s14, s21, ..., s24, s31, ..., s34. We then consider the flattened vector (x, y, s) for the
17


--- Page 18 ---
QUBO formulation. The QUBO matrix for (10) is given by:
Q1 =

The offset for the QUBO formulation (13) is c = 28107. We observe that the QUBO matrix
for the aggregated formulation (10) Q1 and the disaggregated formulation (11) Q2 look quite
different. Notably, the matrix Q1 contains fewer nonzero entries than Q2, since in the QUBO
formulation (12), the variables y and z appear together in a single penalty term, whereas in
the QUBO formulation (13) they do not.
3.5 Generalized Assignment Problem
We consider the same general setting as in the previous section. However, in this case,
the location decisions are assumed to be fixed, that is, the set of open facilities S ŌŖåI
is given in advance. The remaining task is to determine the optimal allocation of
clients to these pre-established facilities, subject to capacity constraints. This problem
is known as the Generalized Assignment Problem (GAP), a well-known combinatorial
optimization problem that remains NP-hard [40, 41].
The GAP can be formulated as the following optimization problem:
min
x
X
iŌłłS
X
jŌłłJ
cijxij
(14a)
s.t.
X
iŌłłS
xij = 1,
ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
(14b)
X
jŌłłJ
djxij Ōēżqi,
ŌłĆi ŌłłS,
(14c)
18


--- Page 19 ---
xij Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆi ŌłłS, j ŌłłJ.
(14d)
The objective function minimizes the total assignment cost of clients to facilities,
where cij denotes the cost of serving client j ŌłłJ from facility i ŌłłS. The first con-
straint ensures that each client is assigned to exactly one facility, while the second
enforces the capacity limits qi of the facilities.
QUBO formulation: To transform (14) into a QUBO, we follow the same procedure
as for the FCFLP. Specifically, we introduce integer slack variables ui Ōłł{0, ..., qi} for
each i ŌłłS to convert the capacity constraints into equality constraints of the form:
P
jŌłłJ djxij + ui = qi, for all i ŌłłS. Each integer slack variable ui is represented in
binary form as ui = PŌäōi
k=1 2kŌłÆ1zik, where Ōäōi = Ōīłlog2(qi + 1)Ōīēand zik Ōłł{0, 1}.
This yields the following cost Hamiltonian for (14):
H =

Lemma 4 The GAP can be formulated as a QUBO with nGAP = |S||J| + P
iŌłłSŌīłlog2(qi +
1)Ōīēbinary variables. Therefore, the QUBO formulation (15) can be solved on a gate-based
Quantum Computer with nGAP physical qubits via QAOA or WS-QAOA.
Proof The QUBO formulation (15) uses the binary variables xij for i ŌłłS, j ŌłłJ, and
the auxiliary binary variables zik for i ŌłłS, k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(qi + 1)Ōīē. This gives a total of
nGAP = |S||J| + P
iŌłłSŌīłlog2(qi + 1)Ōīēbinary variables, and the second part of the statement
follows from Proposition 1.
Ō¢Ī
Corollary 5 Let the penalty parameter in (15) be P = P
iŌłłS
P
jŌłłJ cij +1. Then, any global
minimizer of (15) yields a global minimizer of (14).
Proof For the GAP, the objective function of its equivalent formulation of the form (3), used
to construct the QUBO (15), is P
iŌłłS
P
jŌłłJ cijxij. Hence, the statement is a consequence of
Corollary 1.
Ō¢Ī

3.6 Discrete Ordered Median Problem
The Discrete Ordered Median Problem (DOMP) constitutes a powerful and unify-
ing framework in discrete location analysis, encompassing a wide range of classical
models such as the p-median, p-center, and uncapacitated facility location probelms.
Originally introduced in [42], the DOMP extends these formulations by incorporat-
ing rank-dependent weights into the objective function, thereby enabling an easy
transition between different location criteria. In contrast to traditional formulations
that minimize either the total or the maximum service cost, the DOMP minimizes an
ordered weighted average of allocation costs, where each cost is weighted according
to its rank in the ordered list of client-facility distances [43].
This rank-based structure captures decision-makerŌĆÖs preferences between efficiency
and equity, enabling a controlled trade-off between minimizing average service costs
and reducing disparities in service quality among clients. Such modeling flexibility
makes the DOMP particularly well-suited for strategic planning contexts, such as
logistics network design, public facility location, and emergency service systems,
where both performance and fairness considerations are essential [44, 45].
From a mathematical perspective, the DOMP introduces a complex combinatorial
structure arising from the embedded sorting operation in the objective function. This
interdependence between location, allocation and ranking renders the problem signfi-
cantly more difficult to solve in practice than classical discrete location models. Early
formulations combined integer location and assignment constraints with additional
permutation variables to enforce the cost ordering, resulting in integer nonlinear
programs. In the following, we consider one of the foundational formulations from
this early line of research from [44].
Formally, let xj Ōłł{0, 1} indicate whether a facility is located at j ŌłłJ, and let
yij Ōłł{0, 1} indicate whether the demand of client i ŌłłI is served by a facility at
site j. Again, we assume that I and J coincide, and we assume I = J = {1, ..., M}.
To capture the rank-dependent cost structure of the DOMP, we also introduce the
binary variable sij Ōłł{0, 1}, which equals 1 if the cost of supplying client j is the i-th
smallest among all clients, and 0 otherwise. These variables encode the ordering of
clients by increasing service cost.
The classical N-Median Problem is modeled using the standard constraints: each
facility is either open or closed, exactly N facilities are selected, each client is assigned
to exactly one facility, and assignment is only possible to open facilities:
M
X
j=1
xj = N,
M
X
j=1
yij = 1,
ŌłĆi ŌłłI,
yij Ōēżxj,
ŌłĆi ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ.
20


--- Page 21 ---
The ranking variables sij are constrained to define a valid permutation of the clients:
M
X
i=1
sij = 1, ŌłĆj ŌłłJ,
M
X
j=1
sij = 1, ŌłĆi ŌłłI.
Moreover, to ensure the clients are ordered by increasing allocation cost, the following
monotonicity constraints are imposed for i = 1, ..., M ŌłÆ1:
M
X
k=1
sik

The DOMP objective weighs the clientsŌĆÖ service costs c Ōēź0 according to the chosen
rank-dependent coefficients ╬╗i:
M
X

’ŻČ
’ŻĖ╬╗i.
We note that choosing the parameter ╬╗ = (1, 1, ..., 1) makes the DOMP equivalent to
the N-Median Problem, whereas ╬╗ = (0, ..., 0, 1) makes the DOMP equivalent to the
N-Center Problem.
Together, these constraints and the objective define the DOMP as a nonlinear integer
program with a nontrivial combinatorial structure arising from the embedded sorting
of service costs. The DOMP is now given by:
min
x,y,s

(16i)
QUBO formulation: Let SC denote the sum of all entries of the cost matrix c,
i.e., SC = PM
i=1
PM
j=1 cij. To transform the monotonicity constraints into equality
constraints, we introduce slack variables vi Ōłł{0, ..., SC} for each i = 1, ..., M ŌłÆ1. To
obtain a binary representation, each slack variable vi is encoded using Ōīłlog2(SC + 1)Ōīē
binary variables vik Ōłł{0, 1}, with vi = PŌīłlog2(SC+1)Ōīē
k=1
2kŌłÆ1vik.
In a general PUBO (polynomial unconstrained binary optimization) formulation, one
could directly impose the penalty term for the monotonicity constraints:
P
MŌłÆ1
X
i=1

X
k=1

X
k=1
2kŌłÆ1vik
!2
.
However, this is a polynomial of degree four and therefore not directly suitable for a
QUBO formulation.
To reduce the degree to quadratic, we introduce auxiliary variables {0, 1} Ōłŗuikj =
sikykj for all i, k, j = 1, ..., M. The coupling uikj = sikykj could be enforced via the
penalty term:
P
M
X
i=1
M
X
k=1
M
X
j=1
(uikj ŌłÆsikykj)2,
which is again quartic in its current form. Exploiting that all variables are binary
variables, we replace the constraint uikj = sikykj with the McCormick inequalities
[46], which are tight in this case and given by:
uikj Ōēżsik,
uikj Ōēżykj,
uikj Ōēźsik + ykj ŌłÆ1.
Finally, the inequality uikj
Ōēź
sik + ykj ŌłÆ1 is transformed into an equality
using a discrete slack variable wikj Ōłł{0, 1, 2}. Binary encoding is achieved via
wikj,1, wikj,2 Ōłł{0, 1} with wikj = wikj,1 + 2wikj,2. The factor 2 reduces symmetry,
which may improve the performance of quantum optimization algorithms.
Altogether, this yields the cost Hamiltonian:
H =

,
(17h)
where ŌäōSC = Ōīłlog2(SC + 1)Ōīē.
Lemma 5 The DOMP can be formulated as a QUBO with nDOMP = M + 2M2 + 3M3 +
(M ŌłÆ1)Ōīłlog2(SC + 1)Ōīēbinary variables, where SC = PM
i=1
PM
j=1 cij. Therefore, the QUBO
formulation (17) can be solved on a gate-based Quantum Computer with nDOMP physical
qubits via QAOA or WS-QAOA.
Proof For the QUBO formulation (17), in addition to the binary variables xj for j = 1, ..., M,
yij for i, j = 1, ..., M, and sij for i, j = 1, ..., M, the auxiliary binary variables vik for
i = 1, ..., MŌłÆ1, k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(SC+1)Ōīē, with SC = PM
i=1
PM
j=1 cij, uikj for i, k, j = 1, ..., M,
and wikjt for i, k, j = 1, ..., M, t Ōłł{1, 2} are used. This results in a total of nDOMP =
M + 2M2 + 3M3 + (M ŌłÆ1)Ōīłlog2(SC + 1)Ōīēbinary variables, and the second part of the
statement is a consequence of Proposition 1.
Ō¢Ī
Corollary 6 Let the penalty parameter in (17) be P = PM
i=1

k=1
PM
j=1 ╬╗ickj + 1. Then,
any global minimizer of (17) yields a global minimizer of (16).
Proof For the DOMP, the objective function of its equivalent formulation of the form (3),
used to construct the QUBO (17), is PM
i=1

k=1
PM
j=1 ╬╗ickjuikj. Hence, the statement
follows from Proposition 1.

23


--- Page 24 ---
3.7 Discrete Multi-Period Facility Location Problem
We consider the p-Median Problem, and extend it to a multi-period setting [47]. Con-
sider a set of demand nodes J that must be served over a finite planning horizon
T. Facilities can be located at a subset of these nodes, denoted by I ŌŖåJ. In each
period, exactly p facilities are located at a subset of these nodes, denoted by I ŌŖåJ.
The objective is to determine in every period where to locate the facilities and how to
assign demand nodes to them so that the total cost of operation, opening, and closing
is minimized.
The model parameters are defined as follows. cijt denotes the cost of allocating demand
node j ŌłłJ to facility i ŌłłI in period t ŌłłT. git and hit represent, respectively, the
opening and closing cost of facility i ŌłłI in period t ŌłłT. mt specifies the maximum
number of facilities that can be opened in each period t ŌłłT. When I = J, the model
reduces to the classical multi-period p-Median Problem. We introduce the following
decision variables:
ŌĆó xijt = 1, if demand node j ŌłłJ is served by facility i ŌłłI in period t ŌłłT, and 0
otherwise. In particular, xiit = 1 indicates that a facility operates at site i in period
t.
ŌĆó zŌĆ▓
it Ōłł{0, 1}: 1, if a facility is opened at location i ŌłłI in period t ŌłłT, and 0
otherwise.
ŌĆó zŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
it Ōłł{0, 1}: 1, if a facility is closed at location i ŌłłI in period t ŌłłT, and 0 otherwise.
The Discrete Multi-Period Facility Location (p-Median) Problem (DMPFLP), intro-
duced by [48], is given by the following optimization problem:
min
x,zŌĆ▓,zŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
X
tŌłłT
X
iŌłłI
X
jŌłłJ
cijtxijt +
X
tŌłłT
X
iŌłłI
gitzŌĆ▓
it +
X
tŌłłT
X
iŌłłI
hitzŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
it
(18a)
s.t.
X
iŌłłI
xijt = 1,
ŌłĆt ŌłłT, j ŌłłJ,
(18b)
X
jŌłłJ
xijt Ōēż|J|xiit,
t ŌłłT, i ŌłłI,
(18c)
X
iŌłłI
xiit = p,
ŌłĆt ŌłłT,
(18d)
X
iŌłłI
zŌĆ▓
it Ōēżmt,
ŌłĆt ŌłłT,
(18e)
xiit ŌłÆxii,tŌłÆ1 + zŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
i,tŌłÆ1 ŌłÆzŌĆ▓
it = 0, ŌłĆt ŌłłT\{1}, i ŌłłI,
(18f)
xijt Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆt ŌłłT, i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ,
(18g)
zŌĆ▓
it, zŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
it Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆt ŌłłT, i ŌłłI.
(18h)
In this formulation, facilities can be opened or closed at the beginning and end of
each period, respectively. The parameter mt limits the number of facilities that can
be opened during period t, while the binary variables zŌĆ▓
it and zŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
it indicate opening and
closing decisions.
This formulation allows facilities to be opened and closed multiple times across the
24


--- Page 25 ---
planning horizon, a feature that may be unrealistic in many applications. We refer to
[47] for further modeling extensions.
QUBO-formulation: To reformulate the problem as a QUBO, we first transform
the second constraint into equality constraints. We introduce integer slack variables
yit Ōłł{0, ..., |J|} for all t ŌłłT, i ŌłłI. Each slack variable yit is expressed through
a logarithmic binary encoding, yit = PŌīłlog2(|J|+1)Ōīē
k=1
2kŌłÆ1yitk, and yitk
Ōłł{0, 1}
for k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(|J| + 1)Ōīē. Similarly, we introduce an integer slack variable
ut Ōłł{0, ..., mt} for each t ŌłłT to transform the fourth constraint into an equality
constraint. Each slack varaible ut is again represented via logarithmic encoding,
ut = PŌīłlog2(mt+1)Ōīē
k=1
2kŌłÆ1utk, with utk Ōłł{0, 1}, for k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(mt + 1)Ōīē.
Altogether, these transformations yield the following cost Hamiltonian, which
represents the QUBO formulation of (18):
H =

Lemma 6 The DMPFLP can be formulated as a QUBO with nDMP F LP = |I||J||T| +
2|I||T| + |I||T|Ōīłlog2(|J| + 1)Ōīē+ P
tŌłłT Ōīłlog2(mt + 1)Ōīēbinary variables. Therefore, the QUBO
formulation (19) can be solved on a gate-based Quantum Computer with nDMP F LP physical
qubits via QAOA or WS-QAOA.
Proof For the QUBO formulation (19), the binary variables xijt for t ŌłłT, i ŌłłI, j ŌłłJ, zŌĆ▓
it
for t ŌłłT, i ŌłłI, and zŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓
it Ōłł{0, 1} for t ŌłłT, i Ōłł, together with the auxiliary binary variables
yitk for t ŌłłT, i ŌłłI, k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(|J| + 1)Ōīēand utk for t ŌłłT, k = 1, ..., Ōīłlog2(mt + 1)Ōīēare
used. The second part of the statement follows from Proposition 1.
Ō¢Ī

7 Let the penalty parameter in
(19) be P

Proof For the DMPFLP, the objective function of the equivalent formulation of the form

Ō¢Ī
4 Numerical Experiments
This section presents a comprehensive computational study of the proposed QUBO
formulations for the p-Median Problem, (7), and the Fixed-Charge Facility Location
Problem, (12) and (13), evaluating the performance of both quantum and classical
solution approaches. All computations were conducted on a machine equipped with
an Intel Core Ultra 7 225U processor (2.00 GHz) and 32GB of RAM. The implemen-
tations were carried out in Python 3.11.6.
In addition to the quantum algorithms QAOA (Section 2.2) and WS-QAOA (Section
2.3), we applied classical heuristics to the corresponding QUBO instances to provide
performance benchmarks. Specifically, we considered the following classical heuristics:
1. Simulated Annealing: Simulated Annealing is a stochastic metaheuristic inspired
by the physical annealing process in metallurgy, introduced by [49]. Starting from
a random initial solution, the algorithm iteratively explores neighboring solutions.
Moves that improve the objective are always accepted, whereas worsening moves
are accepted with a probability that decreases over time, enabling the algorithm to
escape local minima. We employed the D-Wave implementation [50] for QUBOs.
2. Tabu Search: Tabu Search is a deterministic local-search heuristic, introduced
in [51]. It explores the solution space while maintaining a dynamic tabu list that
prevents cycling by prohibiting recently visited solutions. Starting from an initial
solution, at each iteration, the algorithm selects the best neighbor not prohibited
by the tabu list, thereby enabling exploration beyond local optimality. We used the
implementation provided via D-Wave [50] for QUBOs.
We use the following notation to refer to the individual methods and parameter
settings:
ŌĆó Gurobi: Exact solution of the original mixed-integer (nonlinear) programming
formulation using Gurobi 12.0.3 [52] via gurobipy 12.0.3.
ŌĆó SA-20/100/500: Simulated Annealing with parameter num reads = 20, 100, and
500, respectively.
ŌĆó Tabu-0/50/250: Tabu Search with parameter num restarts = 0, 50, and 250,
respectively.
ŌĆó QAOA-1/2/3:
Qiskit
QAOA
with
one,
two,
or
three
layers
using
QiskitŌĆÖs
AerSimulator.
26


--- Page 27 ---
ŌĆó WS-QAOA-1/2/3: Warm-Start QAOA with one, two, or three layers using QiskitŌĆÖs
AerSimulator.
ŌĆó Qrisp-1/2/3: QAOA with one, two, or three layers, implemented using QrispŌĆÖs
internal solve QUBO method and simulated using QiskitŌĆÖs AerSimulator.
All quantum experiments were implemented using the IBM software package Qiskit
[18, 19], specifically qiskit 2.2.3 [18], together with qiskit-optimization 0.7.0,
qiskit-algorithms 0.4.0, and qiskit-aer 0.17.2 as well as Qrisp [53] via
qrisp 0.7.13. For the methods QAOA-1/2/3 and WS-QAOA-1/2/3 we used the clas-
sical optimizer COBYLA [54ŌĆō56] with maxiter = 50 and tol = 0.0001. The Qiskit
PassManager was configured with optimization level = 1.
Because the objective of this computational study is to evaluate the algorithmic prop-
erties of the proposed QUBO formulations and the quantum optimization algorithms,
we use the Qiskit AerSimulator in an ideal, noise-free configuration. This allows us
to isolate the intrinsic performance of the quantum algorithms without confounding
hardware noise, which currently limits the size and reliability of experiments on real
quantum devices [15ŌĆō17]. Consequently, the results should be interpreted as proof-of-
concept demonstrations rather than hardware-dependent performance benchmarks.
We focus primarily on the quality of the sampled solutions, specifically, their fea-
sibility, their ratio of the obtained objective value to the global optimum, and the
probability of sampling high-quality or optimal solutions.
All quantum algorithms were executed with shots = 8000, meaning that each quan-
tum circuit was run and measured 8000 times. For QAOA-1/2/3, we used the QAOA
method provided by qiskit-optimization. For WS-QAOA-1/2/3, we also used the
QAOA method provided by qiskit-optimization, but manually constructed the
initial state and mixer quantum circuit following [14] and the procedure in Section
2.3. The parameter ╬Ą, controlling the radius of the ŌäōŌł×-norm ball around ( 1
2, ..., 1
2),
was set to ╬Ą = 0.1, corresponding to a radius of 0.4.
For Qrisp-1/2/3, we used the internal solve QUBO method from Qrisp using qrisp
0.7.13 [53]. As backend, we selected QiskitBackend with the Qiskit AerSimulator,
and we set max iter = 50.
The AerSimulator backends for QAOA-1/2/3 and WS-QAOA-1/2/3 were instantiated
using the SamplerV2 from qiskit-aer interface, with seed = 123, ensuring repro-
ducibility and consistent sampling across experiments. For both methods QAOA-1/2/3
and WS-QAOA-1/2/3, the initial point for the variational parameters is ╬▓i = ŽĆ
4 and
╬│i = ŽĆ
8 for i = 1, ..., p, where p denotes the number of layers.
Both classical heuristics, Simulated Annealing and Tabu Search, were applied directly
to the QUBO formulations using dimod 0.12.21, dwave-system 1.34.0, and the
dwave-ocean-sdk 9.1.0 [50].
In the course of the numerical investigation of the quantum algorithm WS-QAOA,
27


--- Page 28 ---
we introduce several warm-start strategies in Section 4.2. There, we consider opti-
mization problems of the following types. First, we address a nonlinear optimization
problem with simple box constraints; that is, we minimize a function that is at
least twice continuously differentiable over a set defined solely by lower and upper
bounds on the decision variables. Second, we solve semidefinite programs (SDPs).
For the aforementioned type of nonlinear optimization problem, we use the L-BFGS-B
algorithm [57, 58] from SciPy [55] via scipy 1.15.3. The SDPs are modeled using
Picos 2.6.2 [59], and we use the commercial solver MOSEK 11.0.30 [60] to compute
their solutions. For both solvers, we use the respective default settings.
Each QUBO instance was provided in the canonical form xT Qx + c, where Q denotes
the upper-triangular coefficient matrix and c the scalar offset. Linear coefficients qT x
in (1) were incorporated in the diagonal entries of Q, using qixi = Qiix2
i = Qiixi,
since x2
i = xi for binary xi Ōłł{0, 1}. The penalty parameters were selected as
described in the Corollaries 2, and 4 in Section 3.
4.1 Test instances
Since we consider only very small test instances with I = {1, ..., n}, n = 3 and n = 4
for the p-Median Problem, and n = 3 for the FCFLP, we directly report the complete
instance data. All instances are constructed according to the prinicple that assignments
costs cij Ōēź0 for i ╠Ė= j may take values in a large range, whereas self-service costs
cii Ōēź0 are substantially smaller or even zero, reflecting the reduced cost when a
facility serves a client at the same location.
The full numerical data for all test instances are provided in the Tables 2, 3, and
4. In these tables, vectors and matrices are represented using bracket notation. A
vector v ŌłłRn is written as [v1, ..., vn]. A matrix A ŌłłRm├Śn is represented as a nested
list of its rows, [[a11, ..., a1n], [a21, ..., a2n], ..., [am1, ..., amn]], where the i-th inner list
corresponds to the i-th row of the matrix.
Table 2: p-Median Problem: data for n = 3

Assignment cost c

formulations (12) and (13) of the FCFLP, 24 qubits are needed for all test instances
listed in Table 4, except for test instance 4, which requires 25 qubits.
4.2 Warm-Starting
In this section, we describe the procedure for generating warm-starting points for
WS-QAOA (WS-QAOA-1/2/3). The general idea follows the same approach outlined in
Section 2.3 and [14]: we first compute a good point, and then project this point onto
the ŌäōŌł×-norm ball centered at ( 1
2, ..., 1
2) with an appropriate radius.
In the following, we present four strategies for warm-starting the WS-QAOA algo-
rithm. The first two constitute classical warm-start strategies introduced by [14]. The
remaining two strategies, based on the LP relaxation of the FCFLP, are novel to the
best of our knowledge. Each of these strategies allows us to compute a good point,
which is then projected onto the ŌäōŌł×-norm ball, as specified in the numerical experiment
setting above.
29


--- Page 30 ---
4.2.1 R: Continuous relaxation of QUBO
The first warm-start strategy consists of considering the QUBO minxŌłł{0,1}n xT Qx+c
and its continuous relaxation minxŌłł[0,1]n xT Qx+c [14]. In general, this relaxation yields
a nonconvex continuous optimization problem, implying that only a local minimum can
be computed efficiently. To obtain a local minimum, we apply the L-BFGS-B method
as described above. Here, we use as starting point for the L-BFGS-B algorithm the
point ( 1
2, ..., 1
2) ŌłłRn. In the following, we append the suffix -R to WS-QAOA-1/2/3 to
denote this warm-start variant.
4.2.2 S: Semidefinite programming relaxation of QUBO as
quadratically constrained quadratic program
For
the
second
warm-start
strategy,
we
first
reformulate
the
QUBO
minxŌłł{0,1}n xT Qx + c , where Q is a symmetric real-valued matrix, as a quadratically
constrained quadratic program (QCQP). The QUBO is equivalent to the following
QCQP:
min
xŌłłRn
xT Qx + c
(20a)
s.t.
x2
i ŌłÆxi = 0,
ŌłĆi = 1, ..., n.
(20b)
The corresponding standard semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxation is given by
inf
Y ŌłłSn+1

 
c 0
0 Q

, Y

F
(21a)
s.t.
Y11 = 1,
(21b)

 
0
ŌłÆ1
2eT
i
ŌłÆ1
2ei diag(ei)

, Y

F = 0,
ŌłĆi = 1, ..., n,
(21c)
Y ŌłłSn+1.
(21d)
Here, Sn+1 denotes the set of real-valued symmetric matrices in R(n+1)├Ś(n+1), Sn+1 ŌŖé
Sn+1 denotes the postive semidefinite cone (PSD cone), ei ŌłłRn is the i-th canonical
basis vector, diag(v) ŌłłRn├Śn is the diagonal matrix with entries given by the vector
v ŌłłRn, and Ō¤©┬Ę, ┬ĘŌ¤®F denotes the Frobenius inner product. We refer to [61] and [62] for
further details on the SDP relaxation of a QCQP.
We solve the semidefinite program by using Picos and MOSEK as described above.
From the optimal solution XŌłŚŌłłSn+1, we obtain the warm-start point by extract-
ing the vector xŌłŚ= (XŌłŚ
1,2, ..., XŌłŚ
1,n+1). In the following, we append the suffix -S to
WS-QAOA-1/2/3 to denote this warm-start variant.
4.2.3 L: Linear programming relaxation of integer program
In this strategy, we compute a warm-start for the QUBO based on the integer pro-
gram from which the QUBO is derived.
30


--- Page 31 ---
p-Median Problem: For the p-Median Problem, we solve the linear programming
relaxation via Gurobi.
Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem: For the FCFLP, we first solve the LP
relaxation of the aggregated or disaggregated formulation via Gurobi, yielding the
solution vectors ┬»x and ┬»y with components in [0, 1]. To incorporate account for the
slack variables in the warm-start, we compute for each i ŌłłI the logarithmic binary
encoding of the residual capacity, given by
Ōīłlog2(qi+1)Ōīē
X
k=1
2kŌłÆ1┬»zik = ŌīŖqi ŌłÆ
X
jŌłłJ
dj ┬»xijŌīŗ.
The warm-start point is then formed by concatenating (┬»x, ┬»y, ┬»z).
Naturally, this warm-start strategy is applicable only when the QUBO arises from an
underlying integer program; however, this is precisely the setting in which most current
research is conducted. Therefore, this LP-based warm-start strategy is transferable
to general integer programs. We solve the LP relaxation using Gurobi as described
above. In the following, we append the suffix -L to WS-QAOA-1/2/3 to denote this
warm-start variant.
4.2.4 C: Combination of continuous relaxation and LP relaxation
This strategy is a combined approach. We first compute a warm-start point using the
LP-based strategy. That is, we solve the LP relaxation, then, for the FCFLP, compute
the logarithmic binary representation of the residual capacity to construct a reasonable
warm-start point including the slack variables, and use this point as the initial point
for L-BFGS-B. The point at which L-BFGS-B terminates is then used as warm-start
point. In the following, we append the suffix -C to WS-QAOA-1/2/3 to denote this
warm-start variant.
4.3 Computational results
To evaluate both QUBO formulation quality and algorithmic performance across
classical heuristics and quantum approaches, we measure solution quality via feasibil-
ity, optimal solution probability, and approximation ratio.
Both quantum algorithms QAOA and WS-QAOA return a finite set of sampled solu-
tions. In the spirit of a proof-of-concept computational study, we select the sample
with the lowest objective value as the representative solution of the method, directly
reflecting the intrinsic sampling behavior of the quantum algorithm with respect to
the QUBO energy landscape. As our focus is on evaluating the algorithmic perfor-
mance of the quantum algorithms and the underlying QUBO formulations, we do not
apply feasibility-repair procedures or post-processing heuristics. Feasibility is there-
fore evaluated explicitly, and infeasible outcomes provide meaningful information
about the sampling characteristics.
31


--- Page 32 ---
For each QUBO formulation and test instance set, we report one table and two types
of plots that summarize the results across the ten test instances:
1. Feasibility table: showing, for each method, the number of instances (out of ten)
for which the obtained solution is feasible.
2. Objective-value ratio plot: a line plot illustrating the relative objective quality
across all test instances, restricted to feasible points. For each METHOD and instance
i = 1, ..., 10, we compute
ri
METHOD := zi
METHOD
zi
Gurobi
,
where zi
METHOD denotes the value obtained by evaluating the solution returned by
METHOD in the original objective function and zi
Gurobi the globally optimal objective
value computed via Gurobi. A value of ri
METHOD = 1 indicates that the global optimum
was obtained by the respective method on instance i = 1, ..., 10. Infeasible points
are omitted and appear as white gaps in the corresponding lines.
3. Relative frequency plot: a grouped bar plot showing, for each instance and quan-
tum method, the relative frequency of sampling the solution that yields the lowest
objective value of the QUBO. In contrast to the previous plot, infeasible samples are
also included here to illustrate the underlying sampling behavior of the algorithms.
Together, these plots provide a comprehensive qualitative assessment of each methodŌĆÖs
robustness, solution quality, and sampling characteristics across problem instances and
QUBO formulations.
4.3.1 p-Median Problem
We consider the p-Median Problem (6) with |I| = |J| = {1, ..., n}, where n = 3, p =
1, 2, and n = 4, p = 2, using the test instance data in Tables 2 and 3.
A complete summary of the results is provided in Tables 5, 6, and 7 (feasibility tables);
Figures 1, 2, and 3 (objective value ratio plots and relative frequency plots).

start points tend to produce slightly inferior solutions. In particular, the relative
frequencies for WS-QAOA-1/2/3-R and WS-QAOA-1/2/3-S are noticeably lower than
those of the LP-based variants.

(e) Relative frequency plot (WS-QAOA)
Fig. 3: p-Median Problem (n = 4 and p = 2)
For n = 4, the situation changes significantly. Classical heuristics begin to strug-
gle in consistently identifying high-quality solutions. SA-20/100/500 benefits from
increasing the num reads parameter. Tabu Search shows a similar effect. The solutions
obtained from WS-QAOA tend to yield better objective values than those obtained
with standard QAOA methods. Notably, Qrisp-3 again yields very high-quality solu-
tions. Both QAOA-1/2/3 and WS-QAOA-1/2/3-R now occasionally return infeasible
points. For WS-QAOA-1/2/3-R, this behavior can be attributed directly to the poor
35


--- Page 36 ---
quality of the computed warm-start point.
Once again, the LP-based WS-QAOA variants perform very well, returning the global
optimum almost always.
We note that for all test instances of the p-Median Problem, the LP relaxation already
produced integral solutions, solving the corresponding p-Median Problem to optimal-
ity. Consequently, the strong performance of WS-QAOA is not surprising, especially
given that the parameter ╬Ą = 0.1, which specifies the ŌäōŌł×-norm ball used to construct
the warm-start point, was chosen to be relatively small.
Nevertheless, although the underlying integer problems are simple, as the LP relax-
ation is integral, this property does not carry over to the QUBO formulation.
Consequently, computing a warm-start point based on the integer programming for-
mulation, when the QUBO is derived from it, is highly advantageous. We additionally
note that, in this case, solving the QUBO is of little practical interest, as the problem
has already been solved by the LP relaxation. These observations therefore primarily
concern the algorithmic behavior of WS-QAOA and the associated warm-start strate-
gies.
Subsequent experiments on the FCFLP illustrate the broader applicability of LP-based
warm-start strategies proposed in Section 4.2.3 (FCFLP) and 4.2.4. In particular,
they deliver superior performance when the LP relaxations are not integral and even
auxiliary variables are required to construct the associated QUBO formulation.
4.3.2 Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem
For the FCFLP, we consider both the aggregated formulation (10) and the disaggre-
gated formulation (11), with |I| = |J| = {1, ..., n} and n = 3, using the test instance
data provided in Table 4.
A complete summary of the results is provided in Tables 8 and 9 (feasibility tables),
and Figures 4 and 5 (objective value ratio plots and relative frequency plots).
Table 8: Aggregated formulation: frequencies of feasible solutions for n = 3

heuristics,

consistently identifies high-quality solutions, with objective values improving as the
num reads parameter increases. Although Simulated Annealing is not a quantum
algorithm, its strong performance demonstrates the general suitability of the QUBO
formulations.

contrast, the LP-based WS-QAOA variants WS-QAOA-1/2/3-L and WS-QAOA-1/2/3-C
achieve the strongest results across both formulations, outperforming WS-QAOA

0.025
0.05
0.075

Test Instance
Rel. freq.
WS-QAOA-1-R
WS-QAOA-2-R
WS-QAOA-3-R
WS-QAOA-1-S
WS-QAOA-2-S
WS-QAOA-3-S
WS-QAOA-1-L
WS-QAOA-2-L
WS-QAOA-3-L
WS-QAOA-1-C
WS-QAOA-2-C
WS-QAOA-3-C
(e) Relative frequency plot (WS-QAOA)
Fig. 5: FCFLP (disaggregated formulation and n = 3)
initialized from both classical warm-start strategies, the continuous relaxation and
semidefinite programming relaxation. We remark that WS-QAOA-1/2/3-S performs
better than WS-QAOA-1/2/3-R. The LP relaxation of the disaggregated formulation
is integral for test instances 4 and 9, explaining the peaks in relative frequencies
observed in Figure 5e.
Notably, both QAOA-1/2/3 and Qrisp-1/2/3 yield higher-quality solutions for the
aggregated formulation compared to the disaggregated formulation.
Overall, the aggregated formulation produces slightly better results across most
methods. For the aggregated formulation, WS-QAOA-1/2/3-C even outperforms
WS-QAOA-1/2/3-L, indicating that the additional use of the local solver L-BFGS-B
effectively improves the warm-start point derived from the LP relaxation and the
binary encoding of the residual capacity.
We conclude the numerical experiments by noting that the LP-based warm-start
strategy introduced in Section 4.2.3 (FCFLP) and the combined warm-start strategy
38


--- Page 39 ---
introduced in Section 4.2.4, which integrates the LP-based approach with the contin-
uous relaxation, constitute two novel and highly promising warm-start strategies for
WS-QAOA. These approaches significantly outperform classical warm-start strategies
(continuous relaxation with local solver and SDP relaxation) for the FCFLP and
demonstrate significant potential for applying WS-QAOA to solve QUBOs derived
from integer programs. We further emphasize that both warm-start strategies can be
generalized and applied to general integer programs and their corresponding QUBO
formulations.
5 Conclusion
We have introduced novel QUBO formulations for classical optimization problems aris-
ing in location science, network design, and logistics, which can serve as benchmark
problems for the development of quantum optimization algorithms and the evalua-
tion of quantum hardware. For general discrete optimization problems, we proposed a
penalty parameter that ensures global optimizers of the QUBO correspond to global
optimizers of the original integer program.
For the Fixed-Charge Facility Location Problem (FCFLP), we introduced two LP-
based warm-start strategies for WS-QAOA that efficiently produce high-quality
warm-start points. These warm-start approaches are broadly applicable to QUBOs
derived from general integer programs. The numerical experiments have demonstrated
that, on selected instances, gate-based quantum algorithms can outperform classical
heuristics.
Overall, quantum optimization represents a promising complementary tool for dis-
crete optimization, particularly as both quantum hardware and algorithms continue
to advance.
Declarations
Availability of data and materials
All data generated or analysed during this study are included in this published article.
Competing interests
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
Funding
The authors gratefully acknowledge funding from the BMFTR under the QuSol:
Quantum Optimization Solver Kit project.
AuthorsŌĆÖ contributions
F.P.B.: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Project admin-
istration, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.
S.N.: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration,
39


--- Page 40 ---
Supervision, Writing - review & editing. All authors read and approved the final
manuscript.
